releases.summary: Promote object-reference detail panels to a reusable adaptive sheet/drawer surface.

audiences.summary: Design-system now exposes AdaptivePanel as the shared responsive sheet/drawer primitive and wires object-reference details through it.
ObjectReferenceHandler supports same-page vs new-page detail opening, rich nested properties, and configurable sheet/drawer behavior with responsive defaults.
Reference interactions can show richer details in a desktop sheet or mobile drawer without custom per-screen logic.

upgradeSteps.title: Reuse AdaptivePanel for responsive overlay choices
upgradeSteps.summary: Replace one-off sheet/drawer branching with AdaptivePanel when a surface should use sheets on desktop and drawers on mobile.
instructions: Import `AdaptivePanel` from `@contractspec/lib.design-system`.
Use the default responsive mode, or force `mode="sheet"` / `mode="drawer"` when product requirements need one presentation.

evidence: Object-reference tests cover same-page defaults, new-page detail opening, rich nested properties, and AdaptivePanel sheet/drawer rendering.

releases.summary: Route design-system mobile menu overlays through the shared AdaptivePanel primitive.

audiences.summary: Header and marketing header mobile menus now use AdaptivePanel instead of direct sheet composition, leaving sheet/drawer primitives behind the shared overlay adapter.
Design-system overlay behavior is more consistent because mobile menu panels and object references share the AdaptivePanel boundary.
Mobile menus keep the same behavior while using the shared responsive panel implementation.

evidence: A direct sheet/drawer usage scan has no remaining app-level design-system matches outside AdaptivePanel and lower-level primitives.
Design-system tests and build pass after migrating Header, MarketingHeader, and MarketingHeaderMobile.

releases.summary: Document AppShell in-app notification adoption and refresh shell implementation prompts.

audiences.summary: App developers now have public guidance for wiring in-app notifications through AppShell without coupling the design system to notification runtime behavior.
Maintainers get updated copy-ready AI prompts that include library-first notification imports, shell notification props, and migration from legacy notification module imports.

migrationInstructions.title: Use the shell notification boundary for app integrations
migrationInstructions.summary: Keep notification contracts and runtime helpers outside the design system, then pass render-ready notification center state into AppShell.
steps: Use `@contractspec/lib.contracts-spec/notifications` for canonical notification contracts.
Use `@contractspec/lib.notification` for reusable schema, channel, template, and i18n helpers.
Keep persistence, subscriptions, delivery, and mutation logic in the host app or runtime layer.
Pass `ShellNotificationCenter` state to `AppShell.notifications`.

evidence: The application shell docs page now covers sidebar, topbar, commands, breadcrumbs, PageOutline, mobile/native adapters, user menu, and in-app notifications.
The code sample shows AppShell receiving notifications with items, unreadCount, loading state, onSelect, onMarkRead, and onMarkAllRead.
The copy-ready prompts now cover greenfield and refactor paths for notification-aware shells.

releases.summary: Add a ContractSpec-native production-grade translation runtime and optional i18next adapter.

audiences.summary: Translation specs now keep stable bundle identity separate from locale variants while the runtime owns formatter-backed ICU resolution, fallback chains, overrides, diagnostics, async loading, SSR snapshots, and optional downstream i18next projection.

Integrators should use the production translation runtime for server, React, React Native, and CLI resolution, and use the i18next subpath only as a downstream adapter with caller-owned ICU formatting configuration.
Multilingual surfaces can now rely on BCP 47 locale variants, ICU formatting, deterministic SSR snapshots, and safer migration away from locale-suffixed translation bundle keys.

migrationInstructions.title: Separate stable bundle keys from locale variants
migrationInstructions.summary: Prefer `meta.key: "bundle.messages"` with `locale: "fr-FR"` over stable keys that encode locale suffixes.
steps: Move locale identity into `TranslationSpec.locale`.
Keep fallback declarations on locale variants through `fallback` or `fallbacks`.
Re-run translation validation after migration.

migrationInstructions.title: Configure ICU formatting when rendering through i18next
migrationInstructions.summary: The i18next adapter exports ContractSpec ICU messages intact and does not make i18next canonical.
steps: Import adapter helpers from `@contractspec/lib.translation-runtime/i18next`.
Initialize a caller-owned i18next instance with generated resources and `keySeparator: false`.
Configure an ICU-capable i18next format plugin before rendering ICU plural/select/selectordinal messages through i18next.

upgradeSteps.title: Adopt the ContractSpec translation runtime for production surfaces
upgradeSteps.summary: Use runtime instances or snapshots instead of legacy simple string interpolation for production i18n paths.
instructions: Build runtime instances from canonical `TranslationSpec[]` catalogs.
Create one runtime per SSR request and hydrate clients from serialized snapshots.
Keep React, React Native, and i18next integrations downstream from the runtime.

evidence: Adapter tests cover resource export, Arabic/RTL/CJK resources, flat dotted keys, real i18next instance initialization, bundle installation, fallback projection diagnostics, SSR snapshot determinism, and collision diagnostics.
Build output includes the public `./i18next` facade without importing i18next from the core runtime barrel.
Docs describe i18next as optional downstream projection and preserve ContractSpec as canonical.

releases.summary: Add an optional ContractSpec-first i18next adapter for downstream interoperability.

audiences.summary: The translation runtime now exposes an optional `./i18next` subpath while keeping the root runtime and ContractSpec registry i18next-free.
Apps that already use i18next can initialize isolated instances or add resource bundles from ContractSpec translation catalogs without making i18next canonical.
Multilingual applications gain a safer migration path from ContractSpec translations to i18next-powered surfaces while preserving locale metadata and diagnostics.

migrationInstructions.title: Import i18next helpers from the adapter subpath
migrationInstructions.summary: Use `@contractspec/lib.translation-runtime/i18next` for downstream i18next projection instead of importing adapter internals.
steps: Import `exportContractSpecToI18next`, `createI18nextInitOptions`, or `addContractSpecResourceBundles` from `@contractspec/lib.translation-runtime/i18next`.
Keep canonical translation contracts in `TranslationSpec` catalogs and use i18next resources as generated runtime interop data.
Configure an ICU-capable i18next formatter plugin when rendering ContractSpec ICU plural/select/selectordinal messages through i18next.

migrationInstructions.title: Keep stable bundle identity separate from locale variants
migrationInstructions.summary: Namespace translation bundles with stable ContractSpec keys and use `TranslationSpec.locale` for BCP 47 language tags.
steps: Prefer bundle keys such as `commerce.checkout` rather than `commerce.checkout.en`.
Let the adapter map locales like `en-US`, `ar-EG`, and `zh-Hans` to i18next language resource keys.
Review adapter diagnostics for namespace, resource, ICU, and fallback projection issues.

upgradeSteps.title: Initialize i18next from ContractSpec exports
upgradeSteps.summary: Use generated resources and init options with caller-owned i18next instances for SSR and request isolation.
instructions: Export resources from canonical specs or runtime snapshots.
Pass `createI18nextInitOptions(...).options` to a caller-created i18next instance.
Serialize the same runtime snapshot/exported resources for hydration when using SSR.

commands: node scripts/biome.cjs check packages/libs/translation-runtime package.json packages/libs/contracts-spec/README.md .changeset/contractspec-i18n-runtime.md
evidence: Adapter tests cover resource export, Arabic/RTL/CJK resources, flat dotted keys, real i18next createInstance usage, resource bundle installation, fallback diagnostics, SSR snapshot export, and collision diagnostics.
Package exports expose `./i18next` and do not expose `./adapters/i18next`; the root runtime barrel remains i18next-free.
Docs describe i18next as optional downstream projection and preserve ContractSpec as canonical.

releases.summary: Add template-aware import mapping with column aliases, flexible value formatting, codec options, client review state, and server audit evidence.

audiences.summary: Maintainers can define reusable import templates that resolve incoming file columns through aliases, normalized labels, and SchemaModel fallback inference.
Integrators can accept partner CSV/JSON/XML layouts with localized number, boolean, date, JSON, split/join, currency, and percentage formatting before preview or execution.
Import review screens can show matched columns, required gaps, formatting summaries, and ignored source columns before users execute an import.

migrationInstructions.title: Add templates to new import flows
migrationInstructions.summary: Existing explicit mappings continue to work; new flows can pass an import template to plan creation, dry-run, or execution APIs.
migrationInstructions.when: When an import has a recommended column shape but users may upload partner-specific headers or localized values.
steps: Define a template with `defineDataExchangeTemplate` or the backwards-compatible `defineImportTemplate` alias.
Add `sourceAliases` and `format` hints to template columns where partner files differ.
Pass `template` to core plan creation or server dry-run/execute calls.
Use the client controller model to show matched, unmatched, and ignored columns.

commands: cd packages/libs/data-exchange-core && bun test && bun run typecheck && bun run lint:check
cd packages/libs/data-exchange-client && bun test && bun run typecheck && bun run lint:check
cd packages/libs/data-exchange-server && bun test && bun run typecheck && bun run lint:check
evidence: `@contractspec/lib.data-exchange-core` exposes import templates, value formatting, template mapping resolution, and flexible CSV/JSON/XML codec options.
`@contractspec/lib.data-exchange-client` exposes template-aware controller/view-model rows and user mapping actions.
`@contractspec/lib.data-exchange-server` threads templates through dry-run and execution and records mapping-source audit evidence.

releases.summary: Add preference-aware DataView collection defaults and personalization adapters.

audiences.summary: DataView contracts now expose neutral data-depth and collection personalization hints while keeping contracts-spec independent from personalization runtime code.
Apps can resolve preferred DataView mode, density, and data depth through personalization helpers and pass plain props to web/native DataViewRenderer.
Collection screens can remember or infer list/grid/table mode and compact/detail preferences without duplicating DataView specs.

upgradeSteps.title: Resolve personalized DataView defaults in the host app
upgradeSteps.summary: Keep DataViewRenderer dependency-free by resolving personalization preferences before rendering.
instructions: Import `resolveDataViewPreferences` from `@contractspec/lib.personalization/data-view-preferences`.
Pass the resolved `viewMode`, `density`, and `dataDepth` values to `DataViewRenderer` as controlled or default props.
Record user changes with `trackDataViewInteraction` when persistence or behavioral insights are desired.

evidence: Contract tests cover data depth, field visibility, and collection personalization config.
Renderer tests cover pure data-depth projection and controlled DataViewRenderer dataDepth behavior.
Personalization tests cover preference resolution precedence, valid-mode fallback, and data_view_interaction insights.

releases.summary: Speed up npm release publishing by removing per-package dist-tag convergence polling after successful dist-tag updates.

audiences.summary: Stable and canary npm release jobs now avoid the repeated dist-tag verification sleep loop after `npm dist-tag add` succeeds.
Release manifest verification remains the final registry consistency gate, while publish logs now distinguish immediate tag verification from manifest-deferred reconciliation.

upgradeSteps.title: Monitor release manifest verification
upgradeSteps.summary: Dist-tag read-after-write convergence failures now surface at the manifest verification step instead of during every package publish.
instructions: Keep `Verify npm release manifest` enabled after stable and canary publish steps.
Tune `CONTRACTSPEC_RELEASE_VERIFY_RETRY_COUNT` or `CONTRACTSPEC_RELEASE_VERIFY_RETRY_DELAY_MS` if npm registry reads need a longer final convergence window.

commands: node scripts/biome.cjs check scripts/publish-packages.js scripts/publish-packages.test.mjs scripts/verify-release-manifest.test.mjs
evidence: `ensureDistTag` returns immediate evidence only when `npm view` already reports the expected tag.
Successful stale-tag reconciliation records `distTagVerification: deferred-to-manifest` without post-add polling or sleep.
Release manifest tests cover deferred publish-time evidence, final tag success, and final mismatch failure.

releases.summary: Move notifications to library-first contracts/runtime surfaces and add AppShell in-app notification affordances.

audiences.summary: Notification contracts now live in contracts-spec, reusable notification helpers live in lib.notification, and the old module remains as a compatibility shim.
Applications can adopt the new library imports without breaking existing module imports, then wire AppShell notification state through props.

deprecations: The `@contractspec/module.notifications` package remains import-compatible for this release, but new code should import contracts from `@contractspec/lib.contracts-spec/notifications` and runtime helpers from `@contractspec/lib.notification`.

migrationInstructions.title: Prefer library-first notification imports
migrationInstructions.summary: Move new notification integrations away from the module shim.
steps: Import notification contracts from `@contractspec/lib.contracts-spec/notifications`.
Import notification schema contribution, channels, templates, and i18n helpers from `@contractspec/lib.notification`.
Keep existing `@contractspec/module.notifications` imports during migration when consumers need the legacy schema contribution module id.

migrationInstructions.title: Connect AppShell notifications through host state
migrationInstructions.summary: Provide notification items and callbacks to the design-system shell without coupling it to a delivery runtime.
steps: Pass the `notifications` prop to `AppShell` with items, unread count, and mark-read callbacks.
Resolve live delivery, persistence, and subscriptions in the host app or runtime package.
Use the native shell entrypoint for Expo surfaces so the same notification state appears through the native header/menu affordance.

upgradeSteps.title: Verify notification schema contribution identity
upgradeSteps.summary: Confirm old module imports keep the legacy module id while new library imports use the library module id.
instructions: Check `notificationsSchemaContribution.moduleId` from `@contractspec/lib.notification` is `@contractspec/lib.notification`.
Check `notificationsSchemaContribution.moduleId` from `@contractspec/module.notifications` is still `@contractspec/module.notifications`.

commands: cd packages/libs/contracts-spec && bun run test && bun run typecheck && bun run lint:check
cd packages/libs/notification && bun run test && bun run typecheck && bun run lint:check && bun run build
cd packages/modules/notifications && bun run test && bun run typecheck && bun run lint:check && bun run build
cd packages/libs/design-system && bun run test && bun run typecheck && bun run lint:check
evidence: Notification contract tests cover operation keys, model names, and canonical `libs.notifications` feature identity.
Notification library tests cover canonical and legacy schema contribution identities plus moved channel/template/i18n behavior.
Module compatibility tests cover all published notification subpaths and legacy `modules.notifications` feature identity.
Design-system shell tests cover unread badge rendering, loading/empty states, and notification callbacks without runtime package imports.

releases.summary: Add first-class FormSpec phone input support with country detection, split outputs, and flag rendering.

audiences.summary: FormSpec phone contracts now expose input, output, country, and display configuration while keeping the schema-owned value model backward compatible.
Host renderers can set phone defaults through `createFormRenderer({ phone })`, while individual FormSpecs can choose object, E.164, or split linked outputs.
Form-rendered phone fields can show country flags, detect countries from international input, and keep single or split controls synchronized.

upgradeSteps.title: Configure phone fields where split values or single-input UX are needed
upgradeSteps.summary: Existing `kind: "phone"` object-valued fields continue to work; opt into new modes through field metadata.
instructions: Use `phone.input.mode` to choose `single` or `split` rendering.
Use `phone.output.mode` with linked path names to write split country/national/E.164 values.
Use `phone.country.defaultIso2` and `phone.display.flag` for country defaults and flag affordances.

evidence: Contract tests cover typed phone field configuration on the public FormSpec surface.
Runtime renderer tests cover linked split output writes from a single phone field.
Design-system tests cover E.164 parsing, country metadata detection, and flag rendering.

releases.summary: Add PWA update management contracts and runtime helpers.

audiences.summary: App developers can define PWA release policy once, override it per release, and consume a standard update-check API from the frontend.
Maintainers get typed contracts, registry helpers, server evaluation helpers, and React prompt state helpers for PWA update workflows.

migrationInstructions.title: Adopt the PWA update-check contract
migrationInstructions.summary: Register the PWA update operation, bind it to a manifest resolver, and call it from the frontend on startup or polling intervals.
steps: Define a PWA app manifest with `defaultUpdatePolicy` and release entries.
Add per-release `updatePolicy` overrides when a release must be optional, required, or disabled.
Bind `createPwaUpdateCheckHandler` to the `pwa.update.check` operation.
Use `usePwaUpdateChecker` in the frontend and keep service worker activation inside the host app callback.

commands: cd packages/libs/contracts-spec && bun run typecheck && bun run lint:check
cd packages/libs/contracts-runtime-server-rest && bun run typecheck && bun run lint:check
cd packages/libs/contracts-runtime-client-react && bun run typecheck && bun run lint:check
evidence: PWA contracts register the update check query, update events, capability, and feature.
Server helpers merge app default policy with release overrides and distinguish required, optional, and no-update decisions.
React helpers expose prompt/blocking predicates and a hook that delegates actual update application to the host app.

releases.summary: Add a shared roles and permissions policy system across contracts, RBAC evaluation, AppShell adaptation, and personalization suppression.

audiences.summary: Contract authors can declare shared static policy requirements while RBAC providers evaluate static, dynamic, and hybrid workspace-scoped grants.
AppShell navigation and personalization adapters can consume runtime policy decisions without becoming enforcement authorities.
Workspace applications can hide or disable unauthorized navigation and avoid promoting denied fields while server-side policy decisions remain authoritative.

migrationInstructions.title: Adopt shared PolicyRequirement metadata incrementally
migrationInstructions.summary: Existing policies continue to work; add roles, permissions, policy refs, and field policies when a contract needs stronger authorization metadata.
steps: Import shared policy requirement types from `@contractspec/lib.contracts-spec/policy` when authoring reusable contract metadata.
Keep server/runtime `ctx.decide` or RBAC evaluation as the source of enforcement authority.
Pass evaluated decisions to AppShell and personalization helpers only for UX adaptation and suppression.

upgradeSteps.title: Wire app-owned dynamic RBAC providers
upgradeSteps.summary: Apps that store workspace-specific role bindings can implement a role-permission source and pass it to `RBACPolicyEngine.evaluateRequirement`.
instructions: Resolve static templates and dynamic role/binding records for the current tenant/workspace.
Mark explicit denies with `effect: "deny"` so they override static/template grants.
Treat provider failure as a protected-operation denial unless an audited break-glass path exists.

commands: bun run --cwd packages/libs/contracts-spec test && bun run --cwd packages/libs/contracts-spec typecheck && bun run --cwd packages/libs/contracts-spec lint:check
bun run --cwd packages/libs/identity-rbac test && bun run --cwd packages/libs/identity-rbac typecheck && bun run --cwd packages/libs/identity-rbac lint:check
bun run --cwd packages/libs/design-system test && bun run --cwd packages/libs/design-system typecheck && bun run --cwd packages/libs/design-system lint:check
bun run --cwd packages/libs/personalization test && bun run --cwd packages/libs/personalization typecheck && bun run --cwd packages/libs/personalization lint:check
evidence: contracts-spec validates normalized role/permission/policy requirements and passes enriched policy context to runtime deciders before handlers execute.
identity-rbac tests cover static, dynamic, hybrid, scoped, expired, source-unavailable, and explicit-deny precedence cases.
design-system AppShell tests cover no-policy identity preservation, hide/disable behavior, nested pruning, and command annotation.
personalization tests cover authorization context propagation and denied-field suppression from reorder suggestions.
